author: Robert Carr
description: A quick skillet steak sandwich with thin-sliced seared sirloin, pepperoncini onions, garlic mayo, melted provolone, dressed arugula, and toasted ciabatta or hoagie rolls.
equipment: Cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan

groceryIngredients.item: top sirloin steak, strip steak, or flat iron steak
ciabatta roll or sturdy hoagie roll

ingredients: 1 1/4 pounds top sirloin steak, strip steak, or flat iron steak, ideally 1 to 1 1/4 inches thick
1 3/4 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1 teaspoon Morton kosher salt, divided
1 1/4 teaspoons black pepper, freshly ground if possible, divided
1 tablespoon avocado oil or another neutral high-heat oil
1 (12-ounce) jar sliced pepperoncini peppers, using 1/3 cup peppers and 1 tablespoon plus 1 teaspoon brine
4 ciabatta rolls or sturdy hoagie rolls, split
2 ounces baby arugula, about 2 packed cups
instructions: Pat the 1 1/4 pounds steak very dry with paper towels.
Season both sides of the steak with 1 1/4 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 3/4 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika.
Let the seasoned steak sit at room temperature for 10 minutes while preparing the sauce and onions.

Make the garlic mayo by stirring together 1/2 cup mayonnaise, 1 tablespoon Dijon mustard, 1 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1 teaspoon pepperoncini brine, 1 finely grated garlic clove, and 1/4 teaspoon black pepper in a small bowl.
Taste the garlic mayo and add a tiny splash more pepperoncini brine only if it needs more tang.
Melt 1 tablespoon unsalted butter in a cast-iron skillet or heavy saute pan over medium heat.
Add the 1 thinly sliced yellow onion, 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/4 teaspoon black pepper.
Cook the onion for 6 to 8 minutes, stirring every minute, until softened and lightly browned at the edges.
Stir 1/3 cup sliced pepperoncini peppers and 1 tablespoon pepperoncini brine into the onions.
Cook the pepperoncini onions for 1 to 2 minutes, until the brine has mostly evaporated and the onions taste bright and savory.
Transfer the pepperoncini onions to a plate.
Melt the remaining 1 tablespoon unsalted butter in the same skillet over medium heat.
Smash the remaining 1 garlic clove with the side of a knife, add it to the melted butter, and swirl it for 20 to 30 seconds until fragrant.
Place the 4 split ciabatta rolls or hoagie rolls cut-side down in the garlic butter and toast for 1 to 2 minutes, until the cut sides are golden.
Move the toasted rolls to a plate and discard the smashed garlic clove.
Wipe out any loose crumbs from the skillet.
Increase the heat to medium-high and add 1 tablespoon avocado oil.
Pat the steak dry again if moisture has appeared on the surface.
When the oil is shimmering, add the steak to the skillet.
Sear the steak for 3 to 4 minutes on the first side, until well browned.
Flip the steak with tongs and sear the second side for 3 to 4 minutes, until the center reaches 125 to 130 degrees F for medium-rare or 135 degrees F for medium.
Transfer the steak to a cutting board and let it rest for 5 minutes.
Slice the rested steak as thinly as possible against the grain.
Toss the sliced steak with any cutting board juices and 1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Return the sliced steak to the skillet over low heat and divide it into 4 loose piles.
Top each steak pile with one-fourth of the pepperoncini onions and 1 slice provolone cheese.
Cover the skillet for 1 to 2 minutes, just until the provolone melts over the steak and onions.
While the cheese melts, toss 2 ounces baby arugula with 2 teaspoons fresh lemon juice, 1 teaspoon extra-virgin olive oil, and 1/8 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic mayo on each toasted bottom roll.
Use tongs or a spatula to move 1 cheesy steak-and-onion pile onto each bottom roll.
Top each sandwich with about 1/2 cup dressed arugula.
Spread about 1 tablespoon garlic mayo on each toasted top roll.
Close the sandwiches and serve immediately while the rolls are crisp and the cheese is melted.
notes: For speed, season the steak first, make the garlic mayo and onions while the salt starts working, then sear, slice, melt, and assemble.
Top sirloin works well here because it cooks quickly and slices well. Strip steak is richer; flat iron is very tender if sliced across the grain.
Avoid very thick steaks for this quick version. A 1 to 1 1/4 inch steak gives enough browning time without turning the recipe into a steakhouse project.
Use sliced pepperoncini peppers from a jar, not sweet banana peppers, because the brine adds acidity that cuts through the steak, mayo, butter, and cheese.
The garlic mayo should taste tangy, savory, and lightly sharp. If it tastes flat, add a few drops of pepperoncini brine before adding more salt.
Resting the steak for 5 minutes keeps the juices in the meat instead of soaking the bread.
Slice against the grain as thinly as possible. Thin slices make a quick steak sandwich feel tender without needing a marinade.
Dress the arugula right before assembly so it stays peppery and fresh instead of wilting.
Serve immediately. The best texture is crisp toasted bread, warm steak, melted cheese, bright onions, and fresh arugula all at once.
title: Garlic Steak Sandwiches
